title: Foundation Layer
objectives: Identify what belongs in the foundation
Avoid leaking foundation concerns into features

sections.markdown: The Foundation layer is the bedrock of an app: cross-cutting utilities and infrastructure — logging, error types, network client, storage, config, DI, feature flags — used by every feature but owning no domain rules.

A stable foundation is why features can move fast. When network, error handling, DI, config and logging are solved once and centrally, every new feature reuses them and looks the same shape — code review becomes easier.
Letting a feature type sneak into foundation ('Foundation depends on `Cart`'). Foundation must know nothing about specific domains — otherwise it becomes a dumping ground and prevents reuse.

flashcards.back: Cross-cutting, framework-agnostic infra used everywhere; no domain rules.
Logger, Result/Failure types, network client, storage helpers, config, feature flags.
Feature-specific domain types or UI screens.

quiz.explain: Cross-cutting infra belongs there; domain does not.

quiz.q: A red flag in the foundation is…

challenges.text: Add a shared Result<T> + Failure type to foundation and adopt in one repo.
Move a duplicated Dio setup into a foundation NetworkClient.
Extract logging behind an abstraction so tests use a fake logger.
Ship the foundation as a separate package and adopt it in two apps.

interviewQuestions: What does NOT belong in the foundation?
How does a good foundation speed up feature work?
How do you keep foundation reusable across apps?
Foundation vs core vs shared — how do you name them?
